Professor William Ayers was denied the right to speak on campus on April 28, 2010. The University of Wyoming opposed the request for a Preliminary Injunction. The First Amendment right to free speech is protected by the U.S. Constitution. Injunctive relief is needed to prevent future violations of the First Amendment. The motion for preliminary injunction was filed on April 15, 2010, and is just as and just as good as the motion for further relief, just as it is just and just and as just as the Motion for Further Relief is. The Motion for Injunction was filed in the United States District Court for the District of Wyoming, and was filed by David Lindsey, of Killmer, Lane & Newman, L.L.P. For confidential support, call the National Suicide Prevention Lifeline at 1-800-273-8255 or visit www.suicidepreventionlifeline.org.
 Judgment -->

The University's denial of a forum for Professor Ayers's public speech is an unconstitutional prior restraint on such speech. A plaintiff in a First Amendment case must meet four conditions to obtain a preliminary injunction. The reason for the presumption is self-evident. First, where First Amendment rights are burdened, there is a presumption of irreparable harm. Second, the other injunction conditions will typically be met. Third, the injunction would not be contrary to the public interest. Fourth, the Plaintiffs have a Particularly Strong Basis for Meeting The Precedent For Meeting The Preliminary Injunction Standard in A First Amendment Case. Fifth, the parties have a substantial likelihood the Plaintiff ultimately will prevail on the merits. Sixth, the plaintiffs have a strong presumption that the injunction should issue.
 Judgment -->

University of Wyoming refused use of its sports complex for a speech by Professor William Ayers on April 28, 2010. The University received threats and information concerning potential violence in connection with an earlier planned appearance by Ayers that was to occur on April 5 and 6. The earlier appearance, which is not in issue here, was cancelled by the faculty member who had invited Ayers. Plaintiffs also filed a motion for preliminary injunction that is now before the Court. To obtain a preliminary injunction, Plaintiffs have the burden to establish the following: (1) a substantial likelihood of success on the merits; (2) they will suffer irreparable injury unless the injunction issues; (3) the balance of equities tips in their favor; and (4) an injunction is in the public interest.
 Judgment -->

The Plaintiff in this case has attempted to join at least 38 individual Defendants in this single action and an additional 9 in nearly identical Civil Action No. 11-cv-10801. In doing so, there is significant risk ofcreating an unfair process that denies individual justice to those being sued. Federal courts deciding filesharing copyright infringement cases have repeatedly rejected such mass joinder of individuals. The Court held that all defendants except the first Doe were improperly joined, and quashed the subpoenas to ISPs seeking to identify those defendants. The ruling has been quoted approvingly in a number of other similar file sharing lawsuits. It is possible to find nearly identical orders issued in districtcourts around the country hearing similar cases. For more details on this case and other similar cases, go to: http://www.law360.com/blog/2010/12/01/federal-court-rules-file-sharing-lawsuit-is-illegal-and-unfair.

Google asks the court to close the courtroom when testimony regarding Google’s sensitive commercial information is offered at trial. The strong public interest in protecting this kind of sensitivecommercial information from disclosure outweighs the common law presumption of public access to judicial proceedings. While there is a common law right of public. access tojudicial proceedings, that right is not a constitutional right and it is “not absolute.” See Nixon v. Warner Communications, Inc., (1978) The Fifth Circuit has rejected other circuits’. holdings that “only the most compelling’ can rebut that presumption. See In re. Iowa, 724 F.2d.658, 6th Cir. (8th. Cir. 1984) (“Where only private. commercial interests are involved, we think the law justifies the steps taken by the District Court to prevent public disclosure”)
 Judgment -->

Google asks the court to “close the court whenever testimony regarding Google’s sensitive commercial information is offered at trial, and to seal all documents and portions of transcripts.” Public access to trials and trial records “is one of the most ancient features of our common law system, predating not only the constitution but also the Magna Carta itself,” the court says. The Fifth Circuit and numerous courts have determined that public access to civil trials is grounded in the First Amendment to the Constitution. The court says Google can’t cite a single instance where this Court has shut the courtroom doors to the public, nor can Google justify the restrictive remedy it seeks. The public interest must be balanced with the privacy interests of Google and the fairness and efficiency of the proceedings.
 Judgment -->

Plaintiff argues that it should have leave to submit a recent PTO reexamination action because this action allegedly found the claims valid on the same grounds that I/P Engine hadasserted in its validity case at trial. The argument is unavailing for several reasons. The only time period for which post-judgment royalties could be in play is the period between November 2012 and May 2013, when Defendants finished removing the allegedly-infringing filteringsteps from the accused systems. The fact that the PTO subsequently withdrewthese rejections in the recent ex parte re-examination action says nothing about whether Defendants were willful or had a good-faith invalidity belief during the relevant November-May 2013 period. Willfulness is a punitiveenhancement to damages under 35 U.S.C. § 284, but an award of post-Judgment royalties is anequitable remedy.

Chesapeake Energy Corporation and the Individual Defendants are seeking to have the suit dismissed. The suit claims liability under Section 12 of the Securities Act of 1933. The claim against the Underwriter Defendants should be dismissed because plaintiff has failed to allege with specificity that it purchased securities from or was solicited by any of the Underwriters. The complaint must allege by a “minimum allege facts” that the defendant either sold the securities at issue to plaintiff or solicited his purchase of them. In Pinter v. Dahl, 486 U.S. 622 (1988), the Supreme Court held that liability. under Section 121 applies to the person who passes title to the. carbohydratesecurities, and extends “to the. person who successfully solicits the purchase, motivated at. least in part by a desire to serve his own financial interests or those of the securities.owner”
 Judgment -->

Chesapeake Energy Corporation and the Individual Defendants’ Motion to Dismiss should be denied. The Underwriter Defendants were sellers under §12(a)(2) of the Securities Act of 1933 because they solicited purchases in the Offering. As alleged in the Complaint, the Underwriter. Defendants merchandised Chesapeake stock by arranging multi-city roadshows in which they (along. with certain of the Individual. Defendants) met with potential investors and presented highly. favorable information about Chesapeake. These allegations are more thansufficient to allege seller status under Sections 12(a) and 12 (a) (2) under the 1933 Securities Act. The U.S. District Court for the Western District of Oklahoma has denied the motion to dismiss.
 Judgment -->

A complaint must contain more than just a repetition of the elements of a cause of action to survive a motion to dismiss. It has to allege facts. A complaint must nudge the claim across the line from the merely “conceivable” to the “plausible” level of plausibility. The well-pled allegations must be “applicable to all the legal conclusions of the complaint’s legal conclusions.” A complaint “requires more than labels and conclusions, and a formulaicrecitation of the Elements of a Cause of action will not do.’” Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662, 679 (2009) (explaining “only acomplaint that states a plausible claim for relief survives amotion to dismiss”)

Mo Hailong and Mo Yun, who are brother and sister, are charged with conspiring to steal trade secrets. Trial for both Defendants is scheduled for December 1, 2014. The Eighth Circuit has recognized that joint trials are almost always proper, and are nearly universally proper for defendants who are charged in the same conspiracy and face no other charges. Separate trials would force the parties and the court to conduct the same lengthy,complicated trial twice, and this result would prejudice the government. The Speedy Trial Act contains the same preference for joint trials that the severance doctrine protects, and time should be excluded under the Act for delaynecessary to secure a joint trial in this case. There is a strong presumption in favor of joint trials, and the presumption is particularly strong where defendants are charged.
 Judgment -->

The Government contends that its problems timely producing discovery in this case, preclude a December 1 trial. The case against Mo Yun involves no evidence of her involvement in the charged conspiracy during any time period in which an alleged theft of trade secrets took place. None of the alleged excerpts of conversations purportedly involving Ms. Mo from 2007-08 reference obtaining seeds other than those that are sold and grown in the marketplace. The Government must acknowledge the enormous gap in time between the 2007-2008 excerpts and the 2011-12 alleged acts of theft. It is a last-gasp effort to suggest that anyone could reasonably extend Pinkerton liability this far. The just and fair way to respect Ms Mo’s right to a speedy trial and not to be unfairly tainted by the evidence not involving her is to sever her case.
 Judgment -->

They seek compensation under the FLSA for time spent in discharge of their job duties. The Tucker Act, the only possible source of this Court’s jurisdiction in this case, was. created to provide a remedy when statutes impose monetary liability upon the U.S. but do not themselves provide remedies. When a law imposing monetary liability contains its ownjudicial remedies, however, the Tucker Act does not apply. The FLSA establishes liability, a cause of action, a measure of damages, and anapplicable limitations, and expressly authorizes judicial review. Because this Court does not possess jurisdiction to entertain plaintiffs’ claims pursuant to the. FLSA, it does not have the right to contest each factual allegation in the complaint.
 Judgment -->

Function Media, L.L.C. v. Google, Inc. et al is a civil case in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Texas. Google has filed a Daubert Motion to exclude certain Testimony and Opinions of Mr. Walter Bratic. The hearing on Google’s Motion is set for January 5, 2010, so that Google can adequately prepare for the January 19, 2010 trial. Function Media opposes this request on the basis that “a condensed schedule in the midst of expert depositions and Christmas so that the hearing can be on January 5 is prejudicial.” The proposed schedule, however, is not prejudicial to Function Media, whose opposition is due before Christmas. Google requests that the Court grant its request.
 Judgment -->

Google has proposed a hearing date on this issue on January 5, at the same time as the currently-scheduled hearing over exhibit and deposition objections. Such a schedule makes no sense given the timing and the expert discovery here. Google proposes to significantly shorten Function Media's time to respond to Google's forthcoming motion, while also placing the response period at a uniquely inconvenient and burdensome time. Function Media intends to file a Daubert motion of its own against Google's damages expert, Mr. Wagner. Google complains that it would be unfair to hear its motion later, because it requires clarity on the Dauberl issues for its pretrial preparation. Google suggested pushing back the various dates for expert reports -- on four different occasions. Google's motion does not seek to require Function Media to move against Mr. Bratic early. But because Google knows that Function Media will not file by December 16, Google seeks a tactical advantage in having its Daubers heard before Function Media.
 Judgment -->

Jordan Hamlett is accused of trying to obtain a copy of tax returns belonging to President Donald J. Trump. Hamlett was interrogated by agents of the U.S. Treasury Inspector General for Tax Administration (TIGTA) and the Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) Hamlett claims he was not allowed to leave the hotel where he was interrogated. The Government must prove by a preponderance of the evidence that the accused made statements voluntarily. The requirements that statements be voluntary is drawn both from the Fifth Amendment right against self-incrimination and the Due Process Clause. The motion is based on all papers and records on this matter in file 3:16-cr-130-JJB-EWD, which can be found at: http://www.courts.org/case/16-CR-130/JORDAN-HAMLETT-MOTION TO SUPPRESS EVIDENCE.
 Judgment -->

The United States opposes Jordan Hamlett’s (the “Defendant”) motion to suppress. Hamlett contends, first, that agents violated his Miranda rights, and, second, that hisstatements were involuntary. Both arguments should be rejected, and the motion denied in its entirety, the opinion says. The Fifth Circuit has explained that Miranda warnings need be given prior to interrogation only when a suspect is in custody. The burden of proving that the defendant was in custody rests with the defendant who bears the burden of convincing the court he was not in custody at the time of the interview. The opinion says the defense should not be allowed to use its Miranda rights as a defense in this case, and that agents were not required to Mirandize the Defendant.
 Judgment -->

The United States opposes Jordan Hamlett’s motion to suppress. The Defendant contends, first, that agents violated his Miranda rights, and, second, that his statements were involuntary. There was no Miranda violation because the Defendant was not in custody at the time of his interview. Miranda warnings must be given prior to interrogation only when a suspect is in custody. Miranda does not apply and no warnings need be given when the suspect isn’t in custody, as in this case. The court asks whether a reasonable person would have felt at liberty to terminate the interrogation and leave. “The reasonable person through whom [the court] view[s] the situation must be neutral to the environment and to the purposes of the investigation,” the court writes.
 Judgment -->

The United States is seeking dismissal of an indictment against an international cybercriminal. The defendant’s motion is based on an “outrageous conduct” defense. The United States properly sought and obtained the assistance and cooperation of the Maldivian government to bring defendant into custody for purposes of prosecution. The Ninth Circuit has repeatedly held that only the most egregious misconduct on behalf of the government can support dismissal, and has repeatedly rejected ‘outrageOUS conduct’ claims. The U.S. is not guilty of any serious allegations regarding the use of excessive force and does not obviously not involve any serious use of force and obviously does not involve excessive force in this case. The court should summarily deny the motion without a hearing. The motion does not meet the “extremely high standard” required for dismissal.
 Judgment -->

Max and Ann Fernandez are sheepherders admitted into the U.S. under an H-2A nonimmigrant visa that allows a foreign national to work in temporary or seasonal agricultural work. As a result there is a comprehensive statutory and regulatory procedure in which complaints can be investigated and addressed. The federal courts have no power to consider claims for which they lack subject matter jurisdiction. The court is a court of limited jurisdiction possessing only power granted by the Constitution and Congress. The courts must “presume that a cause of action lies outside this limited jurisdiction, and the burden of establishing the contrary rests upon the party asserting the claim.”

Securities and Exchange Commission v. Cuban : Doc. 21/21/2009, 21/2/09, 21:21-21:20. A confidentiality agreement alone is insufficient to create a fiduciary or similar relationship of trust and confidence between the parties. If Rule 10b5-2(b) creates potential liability based solely on the potential existence of a potential "trust and confidence," the SEC suggests that the SEC sought to go beyond the O'Hagan articulation of the misappropriation theory of insider trading. The first situation in which a person is deemed to have a "duty of trust or confidence" is "[]henever agrees to maintain information in confidence" in the first place. In the absence of any other facts or circumstances indicating the.existence of a. fiduciaries or similar relationships of Trust and confidence, there can be no insider trading liability based on the. misappropriated theory pursuant to Section 10(b).
 Judgment -->

The Kansas Constitution declares “Marriage shall be constituted by one man and one woman only. All other marriages are declared to be contrary to the public policy of this state and are void” A state may not deny the issuance of a marriage license to two persons, or refuse to recognize their marriage, based solely upon the sex of the persons in the marriage union. Utah and Oklahoma violated the Fourteenth Amendment. The plaintiffs are seeking a preliminary injunction and temporary restraining order against the state of Kansas. They are seeking an injunction enjoining the state from enforcing article 15, section 16 of the Kansas Constitution, Kansas Statutes Annotated §§ 23-2501 and 23-2508. The suit was filed on October 13, 2014, and was served by e-mail on the following: Jeffrey A. Chanay, Chief Deputy Attorney General for the State of Kansas, jeff.chanay@ksag.org; Defendant Douglas A. Hamilton, Clerk of the Douglas County DistrictCourt, dhamilton@douglas-county.com; and Defendant Bernie Lumbreras, clerk of the Sedgwick County District Court, blumbrer@dc18.org.
 Judgment -->

The Court should deny plaintiffs’ motion for a temporary restraining order or preliminary injunction. No case anywhere in the country, state or federal, has held, implied, or suggested that the use of the terms ‘bride’ and ‘groom’ on marriage licenses violates constitutional rights. The relief requested against Defendants Hamilton and Lumbreras is beyond the Court’s subject matter jurisdiction because it is sought against statejudicial officers, who are exempt from preliminary injunctive relief. Because Kansas law considers same-sex marriages to be legally void andunenforceable only a final judgment binding on all Kansas officials can provide plaintiffs with anything more than the appearance of marriage. There is not even a remote possibility of success on the merits on Plaintiffs’ claim for relief against Kansas Department of Health and Environment Secretary Moser.

Gainesville Sun Publishing Co., 9 F.3d 913, 919 (11th Cir. 1994). “[F]actual disputes that are material preclude entry of summary judgment” in Stephens v. Butler, Alabama.
